description: A14FJ - Day 6, part 4: Packing up Kitty Hawk and Crashing Antares

tokens: The crew of Apollo 14 has just been reunited after a successful lunar excursion by Al Shepard and Ed Mitchell.
Now, the clock is ticking on getting their cargo of Moon rocks unloaded before it is time to releaseAntaresand head home.

Their ascent stage will perform one final duty by triggering seismic detectors on the lunar surface to the delight of the lunar scientists waiting to test them with another artificial calibrated moonquake.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There's a big sigh of relief being breathed around here.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: All over the world, there is.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: You ought to try it from up here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Bruce McCandless would have to wait until February 3rd, 1983, almost exactly 12 years into the future, for his first spaceflight onboard Space Shuttle Challenger's STS-41B mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This world and out of this world, too.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Let me - let me clean up over here and I'll be with you.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: When you have a moment, go to P00 and Data for us, and we've got an uplink for you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Houston, are you going to give me the LM - Command Module weight?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Understand you want the Command Module weight now?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Whatever you have; I'm out, ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: CSM is 34727 and the LM is 5103.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The LM's Digital Autopilot (DAP) needs to have the masses of the two spacecraft updated into it, for proper calculation of the thrust needed to accomplish each maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When you get through with what you're doing there, Stu, I do have Sep PAD for you and a - an updated DAP load, but there's no rush on either one.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The Sep PAD contains information on the Command Module's activities during the LM jettison.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'll take the DAP load now.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I have a LM impact P30 PAD for you when you're free.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Well, I guess we'd better get to cracking, troops.
I've got to put your 160 pounds of rocks some place.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Yes, Can't do it until we get the tunnel open.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: I'll be working on that in just a second here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I'm going to be off the - off the air for about 3 minutes here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: All right, Bruce, Go on your P30 PAD.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'm ready to copy P30 PAD.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: TIG147:54:18.90; Noun 81, minus 0182.0, plus 0039.0, plus all balls; H and H are NA; Delta-VR0186.1; 1:15; 012, 176; minus 0181.9, plus 0039-0, minus 0006.0.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I've got your impact P30 PAD.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:This is a burn calculated by Program 30 to change the orbit of the LM Ascent stage to be on a collision course with the lunar surface.

This burn is performed under PGNS control and will use the RCS jets.Time of ignition (Noun 33):147 hours, 54 minutes, 18.90 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, -182.0 (-55.5); Y, +39.0 (+11.9); Z, +0 (0).

The change in velocity is resolved into three components which are quoted relative to the LVLH (Local Vertical/Local Horizontal).HAand HP(Noun 44):The shape of the resulting orbit is not applicable as the spacecraft is intended to impact the surface.Delta-VR:186.1 fps (56.7 m/s) This is the total resultant change in velocity the spacecraft would experience and is a vector sum of the three components given above.Burn duration or burn time:1 minute, 15 seconds.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 12°; Pitch, 176° Unlike a CSM PAD, yaw is not stated for the LM.

Procedures for a lunar landing allowed the spacecraft to yaw around the thrust axis as required for operational reasons.Change in velocity for the AGS, fps (m/s) (Noun 86):x, -181.9 (-55.4); y, +39.0 (+11.9); z, -6.0 (-1.8).

The change in velocity is resolved into three components which are quoted relative to the LVLH (Local Vertical/Local Horizontal).Additional notes - the LM deorbit burn will be performed automatically by the computer with the command sent from Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And you've already got the LM weight.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: If you would, we'd like a few words from you on the subject of the AGS.
We've lost the AGS downlink telemetry and sort of at a loss as to what its current status is.
Could you spare a little time for that?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: It performed beautifully up until the time you asked me to check circuit breakers.
I looked, and the circuit breakers are okay.
I tried to look at it for a backup breaking gate about that time, and found I could not access it.
Furthermore, the ball, the AGS ball, is still at 150 degrees pitch, zero yaw, zero roll and - has been for some time.
All the circuit breakers were in, but I cannot access it to give a self test.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: When was the last time you tried to access it, Ed?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: oh - about - oh, shortly before we hit the breaking gate.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We'd like you to - on panel 16, cycle the ASA and AE circuit breaker - AEA circuit breaker, if you would, please?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Bruce's next troubleshooting advice is to pull the circuit breakers for the Abort Sensor Assembly (the gyroscopes and accelerometers for the AGS) as well as the Abort Electronics Assembly, the actual backup computer that runs the AGS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: In further answer to that last question, Bruce.
It was some - somewhere around AOS, but I don't remember exactly when.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I abandoned the AGS and started setting up the cameras for the docking about that point.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And, if you didn't see any change, we'd like you to take the AGS operate switch - the AGS Status switch and cycle it from Operate to Standby and back to Operate, and if that doesn't do any good, on panel 11, we'd like to close the Commander's AEA circuit breaker.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That doesn't seem to help either, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I - I put in the Commander's circuit breaker and still has not gotten anywhere with it.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Let's skip the AGS and leave it in its present situation and I've got a few items I'd like to read off for return - over and above the nominal return items.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I'd like to read you up some extra return items, if you've got a piece of paper around.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Okay, I'm cracking the hatch now.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: - back the Commander's Hasselblad and recommend that that go in the ISA.
If you want to bring back the LMP Hasselblad, also, that could go in B-l, but we need the Commander's Hasselblad.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: (Laughter) We thought about bringing them both back, but since you said not to, we left one on the surface.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Item number 5, we want both of the LMP's EVA gloves.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And, of course, we're going to bring back the docking probe.
Now, on stowage: the first three items, the tethers and the webbing, can go in the temporary stowage bags in the Command Module.
The Hasselblad in the ISA, which is normal, and your gloves can go in the PGA bag, and the probe up underneath the right-hand couch in the temporary stowage location.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Okay, we've already stowed most of this stuff, Bruce.
The tether - the 100-foot tether is already in the ISA; the LEC waste tethers can go in the TSB and so can the 30-foot tiedown webbing.
The Commander's Hasselblad we can put in the ISA; the EVA gloves are already in the ISA.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We'd like to get the - the tethers, especially the 100-foot tether, out of the ISA, because you're going to be bagging the ISA in a - a contamination bag, and we plan on using the 100-foot tether, the LEC waste tether, and the 30 feet of tiedown for securing the docking probe for reentry; so we'd like - if you can do it without impact, we'd like you to get that stuff out before you put the ISA in the contamination bag.
All the other stowage is okay.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Okay, we'll see what we can do.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Are you back on the line?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I don't want to interrupt, but whenever you're free, I've got your Sep PAD.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Okay, why don't I take it now?
I'm waiting for a hatch check here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, let me know when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: CSM Sep, RCS, TIG, 146:30:00.0; Noun 81, minus 0001.0; Y and Z are all zips; roll, 301, 355, 348; the rest of the PAD is NA; remarks, LM-jettison time, 146:25:00; and the roll, pitch, and yaw for LM jettison are the same as for Sep.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Sep PAD, 146:30:00.00; Delta-VY, minus 1.0, all zips, all zips; 301, 355, 348; jettison, 146:25:00.00; 301, 355, and 348.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:This PAD is for a small burn to separate the CSM from the jettisoned LM.System:The burn will be carried out using the small RCS thrusters.

The system that will control the burn hasn't been specified but is likely the G&N system.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):146 hours, 30 minutes, 0 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, -1.0 (-0.3); Y, 0; Z, 0.
These velocity components are all given with respect to the local vertical frame of reference.Spacecraft attitude at TIG:Roll, 301°; Pitch, 355°; Yaw, 348°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.There are two additional remarks to this abbreviated PAD.
The time for the LM to be jettisoned is 146:25:00, five minutes prior to this burn.
The attitude for the CSM is the same at both LM jettison and CSM Sep.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm ready to copy any PADs you have for me.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I believe we're up to date already on you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I thought you had a call for me a minute ago.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We'd like to get P00 and Accept, if that's convenient with you, and confirm all the Rotational Hand Control Power Direct switches, Off, please.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You've got P00 and Accept and Direct, Off.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: With the Direct switches in the Off position, Stu has disabled the manual override of the RCS jets which is actuated by turning the Rotational Hand Controller to the maximum position.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stu, are the pressures equal in the tunnel yet?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'm about to drop the hatch.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, Ed, did you verify, or Al, the Forward Dump Valve to Auto?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: How dusty was it down there?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We don't have a lot of dust in here, but our suits are sure filthy.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Apollo 14, this is Houston; 12 minutes to LOS.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 144 hours 46 minutes.
In about 7 and a half minutes we'll loose contact with the spacecraft as they go behind the Moon on the 32nd revolution.
For about the next hour or so all three crewmen will be busily involved in getting a number of items cleaned andready for transport to the CSM where they'll be stowed.
Such items as the cameras, rock boxes helment and gloves, and a number of items in the interim stowage assembly.
They also be bringing back the clothes line tether arrangement used to haul items in and out of the LM on the lunar surface, and will also stow the probe assembly in the Command Module.

This probe assembly normally would be stowed in the LM prior to LM Jettison, but it will be brought back so that engineers here on the ground will get a chance to look at it and see what the problem was in those early docking attempts following translunar injection.

The LM jettison is scheduled to occur at about 146 hours 24 minutes, about 4 minutes later the CSM will perform a small separation maneuver, and at 147 hours 53 minutes the Lunar Module deorbit burn will be performed.
This burn will be initiated by remote control from a command sent from the guidance oficers console here in the control center.

Prior to leaving the LM Shepard and Mitchell will verify that it's set up in the proper configuration to maintain the desired attitudes so that we can communicate with it to get those commands into its guidance system, and they'll also punch in the targeted burn information, which will be prepared by the Flight Dynamics Officer and read up to them.
We're now about 5 minutes, 45 seconds from loss of signal and Flight Director Milton Windler is reviewing status of both spacecraft with his flight controllers before we loose contact.
Astronaut Pete Conrad, Apollo 12 commander will meet with newsmen in the large auditorium in the MSC news center at 3:30 PM today to discuss lunar surface operations on Apollo 14.
At 144 hours 50 minutes this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is the end of the recording of conversations in the Lunar Module audio loop as stored in the DSEA tape.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: With 3 minutes and 45 seconds to LOS, both vehicles are Go on all systems.
We'll see you on the other side.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We've had loss of signal now from both vehicles and we'll be reacquiring at 145 hours 40 minutes and at that time the spacecraft, Kitty Hawk will be on its 33rd revolution of the Moon.
At 144 hours 55 minutes, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Conrad is scheduled to meet with newsmen at this time in the main auditorium at the Manned Spacecraft Center News Center.
We'll take down the release line at this time and record any conversations with the spacecraft when we reacquire in about 38 minutes if that's necessary.
At 145 hours, 3 minutes this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control with 145 hours 41 minutes.
We're standing by now for acquisition of the LM and CSM.
On this revolution, the two vehicles docked.

The crew aboard Antares, Al Shepard and Ed Mitchell will be verifying the jettison attitude, the same as the LM is configured to maintain the proper attitude after jettison so that controllers here on the ground can input the proper information to the LM guidance system to initiate the maneuver with - the Lunar Module that will cause it to impact the lunar surface.
They're also scheduled to get out of their suits and transfer into the CSM.

We'll stand by now for any conversation with the spacecraft as soon as we get good communications - at the moment are quite noisy as we wait for antennas to get into get in the proper attitudes so that we get a good solid lock on.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I'm reading you down in the mud, here; but I can understand what you're saying.
How are you progressing on the closeout?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You're coming in 5 square here; so, if you've got anything to say, we might can pick it up.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: For your information, although I don't want to make this a formal flight-plan update at this time, after TEI, there will be no lunar topo, that is, no Hycon-camera photos.
Do plan on taking the - Hasselblad shots, and the visual assessment of targets 1 and 2.
But right after your DAP load at 150 hours plus 32 minutes, you can plan on initiating PTC and then initiating sleep.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I don't believe that will be a hard one to fill.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: I didn't figure you'd fight us on it.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: What are we going to do about this closeout of the LM?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: On the configuration, you may just leave the AGS in its current configuration.
No need to target it or even fool around with it.
And we'll be talking to you as you progress through the closeout.
This does not constitute a Go for closeout.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The Abort Guidance System remains dead, and Houston has given up on trying to revive it.
The LM impact procedure can be performed on primary guidance alone.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'd like you to go through the DAP-loading procedure at the top of page 15 in your LM Timeline Book.
We don't show the 12021 loaded in the DAP.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I haven't - I deliberately held off on that one.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We're showing some RCS thruster firings that maybe aren't necessary.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I'm at the - ready for IVT to the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Stand by, and I'll try and get you a Go.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The thing we're short on is getting the comm up here the way you want it.
I don't -you haven't given me any steerable angles to set on.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, Ed; you can go ahead and configure the comm at this time.
The nominal values in the Flight Plan.
Pitch, minus 40; Yaw, plus 49, are valid.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Houston, your steerable is beeped up, and on SLEW,
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Roger, Ed; the comm looks good to us and now we need RBTs 5 and 6 on both Normal and Backup Feed, over.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That's what I was waiting for.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: You're Go for final closeout; good bye.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Okay, so long; see you on the other side.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, I'm bringing on the Logic switches.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Roger; stand by for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You are Go for Pyro Arm, over.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Roosa now arming the pyrotechnics that'll be used in separating Antares from Kitty Hawk for the final time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I still have Go for jettison on time, 146 plus 25?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: You are Go for a LM jettison on time.
The only thing we see is P47, when you get down to it in the time line just before Jett.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I'll get my pyros - armed In a [garble].
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Say again about the pyros armed.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Oh, I was just saying I was only missing one thing.
I said I got to get my pyros armed, too.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We gave you a Go for...
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I'm on the last two things on the checklist.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We don't mean to be backseat driving.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: That was Stu Roosa confirming plans to jettison the LM Antares at 146 hours, 25 minutes.

We don't have a precise time for the crew transfer to the Command Module, however, Mitchell reported at 146 hours that they were ready for the transfer into the Command Module and at 146 hours, 4 minutes, we gave them a go to close out the LM.

Following the Jettison of the LM, which will give the two vehicles the separation rate of about a foot per second the CSM will perform a small 4 tenths of a foot per second separation maneuver to increase the separation distance.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Can you verify you can get into the computer; or have you verified it?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And we confirm final Sep here on the ground.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And we bid sayonara to Antares.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Our LM control officer reports that the LM came away nice and clean.
The LM deorbit burn is scheduled to occur at 147 hours 54 minutes 19 seconds.

That'll be a 75 second RCS burn imparting a total change in velocity of about lB6 feet per second and leading to a lunar impact of the LM ascent stage at about 148 hours 23 minutes that we'll get an update on that after some initial tracking.
The preliminary target point is latitude 3.5 degrees south and longitude 19.27 degrees west.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Houston, we're through with the LM Sep burn there, and we're maneuvering to the P52 ATT.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Roger, we copy; through with the Sep burn and maneuvering.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We have approximately 10 minutes to LOS.
After you arrive at your P52 attitude, we'll be ready to send you up a - a desired orientation uplink, and have you noticed any dust floating around?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Six minutes and 30 seconds to LOS.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Everytime you talk, we drop the signal.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Stu, you're coming in pretty well now.
We'd like to have you acquire us on the High Gain.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Houston, if you're asking for P00 and Accept, you have it.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We also need the High Gain.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Houston, everytime you transmit, our signal strength goes down, just right in synchronization with your words.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Houston, if you're wanting P00 and Accept, you have it.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: At 147 plus 10 plus 00, we'd like you to maneuver to roll, 026; pitch, 091; yaw, 000, for communications.
High Gain will be Pitch, minus 10; Yaw, plus 183.
High Gain: Pitch, minus 10; Yaw, plus 183; and the time for that is 147 plus 10.
The normal time for the LTC photo PAD maneuver, LOS, Over.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 146 hours 53 minutes.
As you've probably surmised from the amount of noise on the communications circuits during the latter part of that pass, we were having some problems getting a good lock with the spacecraft.
Stu Roosa reported at 1 point that every time we would try to uplink voice communications trim, then it would fade out in signal strength almost simultaneously with that.

We don't have an explanation at this time, but toward the end of the pass, just before LOS the signal strength apparently improved somewhat and the guidance officer was able to uplink some command into the Lunar Module and hopefully was able to get a new reference matrix, which is used in preparation for the Trans-Earth Injection maneuver uplink to the CSM.

On the next revolution, revolution 34, we'll be completing the uplink of commands into the Lunar Module to initiate the preparation for initating the deorbit maneuver, which will cause the LM to impact on the lunar surface at about 148 hours 23 minutes.
At the end of the 34 revolution, and at the beginning of REV 35, the Trans-Earth Injection maneuver is scheduled.
The Flight Dynamics Officer at the present time is computing the ignition time and burn duration and other information needed by the crew to perform that maneuver.
And that will also be passed up at the beginning of the next revolution.
We're now 59 minutes 18 seconds away from the ignition for the LM deorbit maneuver.
And we'll be reacquiring the Command Module, Kitty Hawk in about 43 minutes.
At 146 hours 55 minutes this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, 147 hours, 36 minutes, Ground Elapsed Time.
Some 1 minute, 40 seconds away from acquisition of Apollo 14.
Coming around on the 34th lunar revolution for the Command Module.
And counting down toward the LM RCS burn, which will cause the spacecraft deorbit and inpact the lunar surface.
That countdown clock now showing slightly over 17 minutes.
The lunar impact is now predicted to take place at 148 hours, 22 minutes, 19 seconds Ground Elapsed Time.
At latitude 3.527 south, by 19.152 west longitude the impact will be at approximately 5, 506 feet per second.
The spacecraft weighing at that time about 5, 270 pounds.
The flight path will be a minus 4 degrees to the local horizontal or the surface of the Moon as it impacts, which should scratch quite a gouge across the surface at such a flat angle.
At the time of the Lunar Module impact, Dr.
David Dudley Strangway of the Manned Spacecraft Center Lunar and Earth Sciences Division will provide a commentary on the experiment for the real time data from the Moon.
This will be in the small briefing auditorium in the building one news center.

Freeman of Rice University also will be present to report on the detection of the LM ascent stage engine exhaust by the superthermal ion detector at the Apollo 12 ALSEP, which is somewhat to the west of the present location.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I've got some PADs here, the TEI-34 and 35 PADs.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, we're mighty interested in the first one, anyway.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Let me know when you're ready to write.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I have pen in hand, awaiting your message.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: TEI-34, SPS/G&N; 34720; minus 0.72, plus 0.08; 149:16:04.30; Noun 81: plus 3013.3, plus 1667.4, minus 0340.3; attitude: 180, 000, 000; Noun 44: HA, N/A; HP, plus 0019.0; 3460.6, 2:28, 3436.6; sextant star, 11, 124.9, 22.5; Noun 61: minus 27.03, minus 171.51; 1160.4, 36252; GET for .05g, 216:23:33; GDC Align stars are Sirius and Rigel, 140, 020, 350; ullage, four jets, 12 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:This burn will place the CSM onto a trans-Earth trajectory at the end of the 34th revolution around the Moon.Systems:The burn would be made using the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine at the rear of the Service Module, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):34,720 pounds (15,749 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.72° and +0.08°.

These angles represent an initial direction for the gimbal-mounted engine.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):149 hours, 16 minutes, 4.30 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3,013.3 (+918.5); Y, +1,667.4 (+508.2); Z, -340.3 (-103.7).
The change in velocity is resolved into three components which are quoted relative to the local vertical.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 180°; Pitch, 0°; Yaw, 0°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.

Since the platform will be aligned to match their desired burn attitude, the numbers are simple.HA, expected apogee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):The resultant trajectory will transfer from one world to another so an apogee figure is not applicable.HP, expected perigee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):19.0 nautical miles (35.2 km).
This trajectory will reach its closest point to Earth within the atmosphere and thus will cause the spacecraft to re-enter.Delta-VT:3,460.6 fps (1,054.8 m/s).
This is the total change in velocity the spacecraft would experience and is a vector sum of the three components given above.Burn duration or burn time:2 minutes, 28 seconds.Delta-VC:3,436.6 fps.
Using its ability to independently measure acceleration, the EMS can shut down the engine in case the G&N system fails to do so.

This figure is to be loaded into a counter (hence Delta-VC) on the EMS, and is slightly lower than Delta-VTbecause the EMS does not take account of the engine's tail-off thrust.Sextant star:Star 11 (Aldebaran, Alpha Tauri) visible in sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are 124.9° and 22.5° respectively.
This is part of an attitude check.Boresight star:Not available.
This is a second attitude check which would be made by sighting on another celestial object with the COAS (Crew Optical Alignment Sight) out of a window.

However, during a TEI burn, the windows will be facing the Moon.The next five parameters all relate to re-entry, during which an important milestone is when atmospheric drag on the spacecraft imparts a deceleration of 0.05g.Expected splashdown point (Noun 61):27.03° south, 171.51° west;Range to go from the 0.05g event:1,160.4 nautical miles.
To set up their EMS (Entry Monitor System) before re-entry, the crew need to know the expected distance, in nautical miles, that the CM would travel after the EMS is triggered by the 0.05g event.
This number would be entered into a counter and it would be decremented during re-entry based on data from the EMS's own accelerometer.Expected velocity at the 0.05g event:36,252 fps (11,050 m/s).
This fps figure will be entered into the EMS's Delta-V counter and will be decremented by data from the EMS's own accelerometer.Time of the 0.05g event:216 hours, 23 minutes and 33 seconds GET.

This is the predicted time at which the spacecraft's G&N system would be expected to measure one twentieth of a g.GDC Align stars:The stars to be used for GDC Align purposes are Sirius and Rigel.GDC Align angles:The align angles are roll, 140°; pitch, 20°; yaw, 350°.

If Sirius and Rigel are viewed through the telescope and the spacecraft rotated to align them in the reticle in a certain way, then these angles will represent its attitude with respect to the desired REFSMMAT.

The backup attitude measurement system, centered around the Gyro Display Couplers, can then be set with those angles.The PAD includes an additional note that propellant should be settled to the bottom of the SPS tanks by thrusting with four RCS jets for 12 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: TEI-35, SPS/G&N; the weight is 34720; minus 0.72, plus 0.08; 151:16:02.94; plus 3097.7, plus 1633.1, minus 0468.2; 178, 358, 359; ullage, four jets, ele - correction, four jets, 12 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:This is essentially a backup PAD in case the planned burn at TEI-34 fails to occur.

In a similar fashion to the previous PAD, its intention is to inject the CSM into a trans-Earth trajectory at the end of the 35th revolution around the Moon.Systems:The burn would be made using the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine at the rear of the Service Module, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):34,720 pounds (15,749 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.72° and +0.08°.

These angles represent an initial direction for the gimbal-mounted engine.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):151 hours, 16 minutes, 2.94 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3,097.7 (+944.2); Y, +1,633.1 (+497.8); Z, -468.2 (-142.7).
The change in velocity is resolved into three components which are quoted relative to the local vertical.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 178°; Pitch, 358°; Yaw, 359°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.

Since the platform would be aligned to match their desired burn attitude on the previous rev, the angles are similar.This PAD is very similar to the previous and so only the major items have been read up.
It includes an additional note that propellant should be settled to the bottom of the SPS tanks by thrusting with four RCS jets for 12 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I have some more information for this upcoming photographs with the 500-millimeter of the 13 S-IVB impact crater.

tokens: We'll give you a state vector and target load while we're getting this.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: At 148:15, maneuver to roll, 026; pitch, 104; yaw, 000.
suggest you use magazine Papa, the same 500-millimeter COAS procedures as before.
For locating visually the target, we suggest you use the zero-phase landmark map 7 and 8.
To refresh your memory, the impact crater is on the northeastern rim of Lansberg B located on a line from Lansberg B to Lansberg.
So, these procedures - We want you to pitch at T-start plus 1 minute and ignore the ORDEAL.
Do not use the ORDEAL numbers be - This is because of a misaline-ment due to the TEI REFSMMAT.
The pitch should work out to be a pure pilot pitch.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Use magazine P, the same 500-millimeter procedures.
I don?t know how good an area this type of photography covers, though.

transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I - I thought it - Of course, I sort of got the impression today, Gordon, that - that it might have been narrower than that, but that's fine.
I - I know the general area to photo.

tokens: And I guess you want me to do that after 1 minute, pitch to 328, and quit, huh?

tokens: We're through with the uplink., And LOS time is 148:50:34; 180 time: 149:15:29; AOS with TEI: 149:26:49; AOS without TEI: 149:36:45.

tokens: I think the only one there that - give me the - I don't need the 180; but I got with TEI; AOS will be 149:26:49; without, 149:36:45.

tokens: For your information, there's about 3 minutes to the - 2 minutes and 50 seconds to the LM ignition.

tokens: We took a look at your geometry when you're in this attitude prior to starting the COAS maneuver.
It turns out that you're probably going to be able to see, through the optics, Antares hit the ground.
If you wish and you have the time, one of the LM crew there could select P24, and we've got the numbers to set into Noun 89.
And we'll give you a - an impact time.
You may be able to see it hit.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Right now, we may have an update on this with - after they track it, after the burn but right now, minus 03.527, minus 09.576, and a plus all balls.

tokens: Yes, I copy minus 03.527, minus 09.579, plus all zips.

tokens: That second register, last digit is 6, minus 09.576.

tokens: If it's handy and you wanted to try to photograph it through the sextant, we'd suggest using C-EX at a 24 frames per second, l/60th-of-a-second shutter speed, and start it at 30 seconds prior to impact, and let it run through to 30 seconds after.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And the LM is burning now.

tokens: We'd like you to use f/ll, l/250th, focus infinity.

tokens: This is Apollo Control about 17 minutes away from Lunar Module impact.
Again a reminder to newsmen in the Houston News Center, Dr.
Strangway in the MSC Lunar and Earth Science Divison will be in the small briefing room in the news room, the news center accompanied by Dr.

Freeman of Rice University who will also be there to report on the detection of Apollo 14 Lunar Module ascent stage engine exhaust by the suprathermal ion detector way over the Apollo 12 landing site.

tokens: That Noun 89 and the time of impacts for the - for Antares are good, after looking at it after its burn.

tokens: Gordon mentions here Program 30 - which is used for calculating engine burns - and Program 40 which is used to calculate and perform SPS burns automatically under CMC control.

tokens: We'll just let you look at them early, Gordon.

tokens: We've got about 9 and a half minutes until LM impact.

tokens: 14, Houston, One minute to LOS, and we'll see you on your way home.

tokens: We have had Loss Of Signal as Apollo 14 went behind the Moon on its final revolution of the Moon.
With its successful TEI burn, Trans-Earth Injection, the spacecraft should come out on the eastern edge of the Moon at 149:26:49 Ground Elapsed Time.
At the present time in the Houston News Center, the Maroon team of flight directors, Milton Windler, accompanied by ALSEP's Senior Engineer, Paul Nering, will hold a Change of Shift Press Conference.
At this time the circuit will be taken down and come up again just prior to acquisition of signal as Apollo 14 comes around on its beginning of its trans-Earth coast path.
transcript.speaker: PAO
